blog_text:
The Library of Congress holds books, journal articles and other resources that explore unconventional theories about the shape of the Earth. This post focuses on maps that show the Earth in unusual forms.

Early civilizations held diverse beliefs about the shape of the Earth. Some believed that the Earth was flat or that it was floating on water. Below is a diagram of an ancient theory about the Earth’s form. The diagram was derived from an article by O.C. Whitehouse, a 19th century professor and expert in ancient Hebrew literature. The Earth is represented as a disc, enclosed in a vault and surrounded by an ocean.

The concept of a spherical Earth started to gain acceptance in the third century BC. Aristotle, Parmenides, and other ancient philosophers correctly stated that the Earth is spherical in shape. The astronomer Aristarchus of Samos believed that the Earth revolved around the sun. Aristarchus measured the circumference of the Earth and Moon by observing the Earth’s circular shadow on the Moon during a lunar eclipse. The geographer and mathematician Claudius Ptolemy described the Earth as a sphere with the Sun and planets revolving around it; this concept is known as the geocentric model of the universe.

The idea of a spherical Earth continued to be recognized throughout the Middle Ages; however, there were skeptics. In the year AD 550, Cosmas Indicopleustes, a merchant and writer, described the world as a giant mountain, enclosed in a vault and surrounded by water. Noted geographer and historian Johann Georg Kohl traced this representation of Cosmas’s theory from a print in the French publication Voyageurs Anciens et Modernes.

The fact that the Earth has a spherical shape has been widely acknowledged in modern society, although the concept of a flat and stationary Earth gained acceptance among some individuals in the 19th century. This is mainly attributed to the British inventor and flat Earth advocate, Samuel Birley Rowbotham. Samuel Rowbotham believed that the Earth is shaped like a disc with the North Pole at its center. He named his theory Zetetic Astronomy. In 1865, Rowbotham wrote a book titled Zetetic Astronomy: Earth not a Globe! under the pseudonym Parallax.

Samuel Rowbotham passed away in 1884; however, the number of individuals supporting the flat Earth theory continued to grow. Featured below are two maps that were made after Rowbotham’s death.

In 1893, an American professor named Orlando Ferguson created the Map of the Square and Stationary Earth. Professor Ferguson based his beliefs on a biblical passage which describes four angels standing at the corners of the Earth.

Detailed below is an illustration from the map. Two men are desperately clinging to a spherical Earth as it travels 65,000 miles per hour around the Sun.

Next is a map created in 1920 by another American professor, John G. Abizaid. Professor Abizaid believed that the Earth is flat and stationary with the Sun, Moon, and stars in constant motion. Abizaid shared his theories in his book The Enlightenment of the World.

The number of flat Earth advocates increased during the 20th century. In 1956, Samuel Shenton, a conspiracy theorist and lecturer, founded the International Flat Earth Research Society. As of today, flat Earth research organizations remain active.

I have included some background information regarding unconventional beliefs about the Earth’s shape; discover more through the sources listed below.

Further Reading:

The Flat Earth and Its Advocates, a Library of Congress research guide prepared by Sean Bryant, Science Reference Specialist, Science, Technology and Business Division. This guide is adapted from a 1998 reference list created by Ruth Freitag titled “The Flat Earth and its Advocates: A List of References.” The guide provides links to books and journal articles on the pseudoscientific theory that the earth is a flat disc. The Atlas of Legendary Lands : Fabled Kingdoms, Phantom Islands, Lost Continents and Other Mythical Worldsby Judith A. McLeod. (pages 20-25). The Phantom Atlas : The Greatest Myths, Lies and Blunders on Mapsby Edward Brooke-Hitching. (pages 102-105).

metadata.summary: This blog post from the Library of Congress explores the history of unconventional beliefs about the shape of the Earth, highlighting resources and maps that illustrate these theories. It examines early ideas such as a flat Earth, the Earth floating on water, and ancient diagrams, as well as the rise of the spherical Earth concept in classical antiquity. The post provides a visual and historical overview of how different cultures have represented the world in maps and thought.

accuracy_text:
## Accuracy Analysis

**1. Sources and Evidence**
The blog post provides some sources and references, including specific books and articles. For example, it cites “an article by O.C. Whitehouse, a 19th century professor and expert in ancient Hebrew literature” and references Johann Georg Kohl’s tracing from the French publication "Voyageurs Anciens et Modernes" with a hyperlink. It also references Samuel Rowbotham’s 1865 book and provides a link to the text on archive.org. However, not every assertion is accompanied by a citation or direct data.

**2. Errors, Contradictions, and Inconsistencies**
No obvious factual errors, contradictions, or logical inconsistencies are present in the provided excerpt. The text lays out a historical timeline and describes different thinkers’ beliefs in a coherent and consistent manner.

**3. Facts vs. Opinions**
The author presents information in a factual tone and does not use opinion or hedging language such as “I believe” or “in my view.” Statements such as “The concept of a spherical Earth started to gain acceptance in the third century BC” and “The fact that the Earth has a spherical shape has been widely acknowledged in modern society” are presented as facts. There is no mixing of opinions and facts, nor is there editorializing in the text that is provided.

**4. Verifiability**
Most main claims are verifiable through external, trustworthy sources. Key claims, such as the historical acceptance of the spherical Earth, Aristotle’s position, Aristarchus's astronomical works, Ptolemy’s geocentric model, the works of Cosmas Indicopleustes, and Samuel Rowbotham’s advocacy and book, are all subjects documented in reputable histories of science and are checkable through academic and archival references. The specific references to publications and links further aid in external verification.

facts.fact: O.C. Whitehouse, a 19th century professor and expert in ancient Hebrew literature, proposed a model of the Earth where it is represented as a disc, enclosed in a vault and surrounded by an ocean.
facts.verification:
## Fact Check

**Claim:**  
O.C. Whitehouse, a 19th century professor and expert in ancient Hebrew literature, proposed a model of the Earth where it is represented as a disc, enclosed in a vault and surrounded by an ocean.

**Verdict:** PARTIALLY TRUE

**Justification:**  
Evidence shows that Rev. Owen C. Whitehouse, a 19th-century biblical scholar, did interpret ancient Hebrew cosmology as depicting the world as a disk (“Chug”) with a vault above and surrounded by waters—interpreting metaphors in the Old Testament that suggest the earth was thought of as flat and enclosed by a rigid firmament ([downloads.seaverns.com](https://downloads.seaverns.com/Library/History/Biblical/Encyclopedia_Biblica_V1.pdf?utm_source=openai)). While he did discuss these cosmological images, he did not propose them as literal physical models but rather as metaphorical or poetic representations within Hebrew literature—an important nuance that the claim overlooks ([upload.wikimedia.org](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/f/fd/Books_from_the_Library_of_Congress_%28IA_earliestcosmolog00warr%29.pdf?utm_source=openai)).

**References:**
- Encyclopedia Biblica entry on Rev. Owen C. Whitehouse, M.A., biblical exegesis professor ([downloads.seaverns.com](https://downloads.seaverns.com/Library/History/Biblical/Encyclopedia_Biblica_V1.pdf?utm_source=openai))  
- Analysis criticizing literal cosmological models in Hebrew texts, noting disk world and firmament metaphors ([upload.wikimedia.org](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/f/fd/Books_from_the_Library_of_Congress_%28IA_earliestcosmolog00warr%29.pdf?utm_source=openai))

facts.fact: The astronomer Aristarchus of Samos believed that the Earth revolved around the sun and measured the circumference of the Earth and Moon by observing the Earth's circular shadow on the Moon during a lunar eclipse.
facts.verification:
## Fact Check

**Claim:** The astronomer Aristarchus of Samos believed that the Earth revolved around the sun and measured the circumference of the Earth and Moon by observing the Earth's circular shadow on the Moon during a lunar eclipse.

**Verdict:** PARTIALLY TRUE

**Justification:** Credible sources confirm that Aristarchus of Samos proposed an early heliocentric model, suggesting the Earth revolves around the Sun. This is well-supported by historical records and scholarly summaries. ([britannica.com](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Aristarchus-of-Samos?utm_source=openai)) Moreover, Aristarchus used observations of Earth's circular shadow on the Moon during a lunar eclipse to estimate the relative sizes of the Moon and Earth (and the Sun), but he did *not* measure the Earth's circumference. That measurement is credited to Eratosthenes, not Aristarchus. ([cristoraul.org](https://cristoraul.org/ENGLISH/readinghall/THIRDMILLENNIUMLIBRARY/Biographies/310-230-Aristarchus-of-Samos.html?utm_source=openai)) Therefore, the claim is accurate regarding the heliocentric belief and lunar eclipse method, but incorrect in attributing measurement of Earth's circumference to Aristarchus.

**References:**
- Encyclopaedia Britannica: Aristarchus’s heliocentric theory, and his work on sizes and distances using lunar eclipse geometry ([britannica.com](https://www.britannica.com/biography/Aristarchus-of-Samos?utm_source=openai))
- Wikipedia and other sources: details on his heliocentric model and lunar eclipse shadow method ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aristarchus_of_Samos?utm_source=openai))
- Eratosthenes’s measurement of Earth’s circumference, not by Aristarchus ([cristoraul.org](https://cristoraul.org/ENGLISH/readinghall/THIRDMILLENNIUMLIBRARY/Biographies/310-230-Aristarchus-of-Samos.html?utm_source=openai))

facts.fact: Claudius Ptolemy described the Earth as a sphere with the Sun and planets revolving around it, a concept known as the geocentric model of the universe.
facts.verification:
## Fact Check

**Claim:** Claudius Ptolemy described the Earth as a sphere with the Sun and planets revolving around it, a concept known as the geocentric model of the universe.

**Verdict:** TRUE

**Justification:** The claim accurately reflects Ptolemy’s cosmological model as set out in his 2nd-century work *Almagest*. Ptolemy indeed described Earth as a stationary sphere positioned at the center of the cosmos, with the Sun, Moon, planets, and stars moving in complex circular paths around it—featuring deferents, epicycles, and nested celestial spheres. ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Almagest?utm_source=openai)) The system, widely known as the Ptolemaic or geocentric model, remained the dominant astronomical paradigm for centuries. ([britannica.com](https://www.britannica.com/science/Ptolemaic-system?utm_source=openai))

**References:**
- “Almagest” Wikipedia article: Ptolemy’s Earth is a sphere at the center of the cosmos and does not move ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Almagest?utm_source=openai))  
- Britannica’s “Almagest” entry: Book I outlines a geocentric spherical cosmos with detailed motion of Sun, Moon, planets, and stars ([britannica.com](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Almagest?utm_source=openai))  
- Britannica’s “Ptolemaic system” entry: Describes geocentric model with Sun and planets orbiting a stationary Earth ([britannica.com](https://www.britannica.com/science/Ptolemaic-system?utm_source=openai))

facts.fact: In AD 550, Cosmas Indicopleustes, a merchant and writer, described the world as a giant mountain, enclosed in a vault and surrounded by water.
facts.verification:
## Fact Check

**Claim:** In AD 550, Cosmas Indicopleustes, a merchant and writer, described the world as a giant mountain, enclosed in a vault and surrounded by water.

**Verdict:** PARTIALLY TRUE

**Justification:**  
Cosmas Indicopleustes, writing *around* AD 550, indeed depicted the world as **flat**, surrounded by **ocean**, and enclosed under a **vaulted heaven**—a model inspired by the Tabernacle and featuring walls and a roof-like structure of the heavens ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cosmas_Indicopleustes?utm_source=openai)). However, the Earth was not described *as* a giant mountain; rather, he asserted the **sun** revolved around a **conical mountain** in the far north, which explained seasonal changes in day length ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cosmas_Indicopleustes?utm_source=openai)).

**References:**
- Cosmas’s world is flat, surrounded by ocean, with the heavens forming a vaulted canopy ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cosmas_Indicopleustes?utm_source=openai))  
- The sun revolves around a northern conical mountain, accounting for the variation in day length ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cosmas_Indicopleustes?utm_source=openai))

purpose.style: Academic and informative. The post cites historical figures, scholarly sources, and specific publications, e.g., 'The diagram was derived from an article by O.C. Whitehouse, a 19th century professor and expert in ancient Hebrew literature.'

The treatment of ancient beliefs is factual and respectful: 'Some believed that the Earth was flat or that it was floating on water.'

The style is academic, referencing scholars, geographers, and historical texts.
No bias is displayed; multiple viewpoints are presented evenly, with no derogatory or elevated language toward any group.

No hate speech or offensive terminology appears in the text.

There are no disclosure statements, ads, affiliate links, or sponsored content present.
There is no inflammatory or polarizing language in the excerpt.

publisher_authority.ownership_and_funding: The Library of Congress (LoC) is a federal institution under the United States Congress, established in 1800 and serving as the nation’s de facto national library ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Library_of_Congress?utm_source=openai)). Its operations are funded primarily through Congressional appropriations, with a budget of approximately $802 million and staffing of over 3,100 as of recent data ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Library_of_Congress?utm_source=openai)). Additionally, the LoC supplements its budget through private fundraising: under Librarian James Billington, it created a development office and established the James Madison Council to solicit private donations, raising more than half a billion dollars to support collections, outreach, and digitization ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Library_of_Congress?utm_source=openai)). Transparency in its finances is reinforced by the establishment of an Office of the Inspector General and commissioning Government Accountability Office (GAO) audits, yielding clean opinions since 1995 ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Library_of_Congress?utm_source=openai)). Corporate giving also contributes to specific programs—e.g., sponsorships for exhibitions and public programming—though these are clearly disclosed under “Corporate Giving” policies ([wwws.loc.gov](https://wwws.loc.gov/philanthropy/corporate.php?utm_source=openai)). Overall, LoC displays a high level of financial transparency, with no evident conflicts of interest or undue corporate or political influence.
publisher_authority.editorial_standards: The Library of Congress maintains clearly articulated procedures for error reporting and corrections. For example, it provides an error report form for catalog record corrections, although it notes limitations in responding to requests unless inaccuracies stem from deviation from original cataloging standards ([loc.gov](https://www.loc.gov/catdir/cpso/modification-catalog-records.html?utm_source=openai)). While not a news publisher, the LoC operates with high standards of accuracy, and its Congressional Research Service (CRS) is known for producing impartial, nonpartisan research leveraging the institution’s comprehensive collections ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Federal_Research_Division?utm_source=openai)). Its “Comment & Posting Policy” for public interaction emphasizes respectful civil discourse, moderation, and legal disclaimers, supporting transparency and appropriate engagement through digital platforms ([loc.gov](https://www.loc.gov/legal/comment-and-posting-policy/?utm_source=openai)). These policies reflect robust editorial control, aimed at maintaining accuracy, transparency, and integrity in its informational services.
publisher_authority.political_bias: As a legislative research library and cultural institution under federal oversight, the LoC is institutionally designed to be politically neutral, serving Congress across the political spectrum and the general public. The Congressional Research Service, in particular, is explicitly characterized as objective and non-partisan ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Federal_Research_Division?utm_source=openai)). No credible assessments by media watchdogs suggest any ideological bias. This alignment with its statutory mission supports a conclusion of minimal political bias.
publisher_authority.factual_reliability: The Library of Congress is widely regarded, both academically and institutionally, for its reliability. It is home to unparalleled collections—millions of books, manuscripts, maps, recordings, and rare documents—and its CRS produces authoritative research ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Library_of_Congress?utm_source=openai)). As a federal entity with oversight and regular audits by GAO and the Inspector General, its operational and factual practices are held to rigorous scrutiny ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Library_of_Congress?utm_source=openai)). Though occasionally discussions (e.g., cataloging decisions) prompt community feedback, as in debates over discontinuing certain catalog subdivisions, the institution’s transparent engagement and documentation of those processes bolster credibility ([reddit.com](https://www.reddit.com/r/Libraries/comments/1q5xs4q/response_to_the_library_of_congress_genreform/?utm_source=openai)).
publisher_authority.public_perception:
The Library of Congress enjoys a sterling reputation among scholars, librarians, and the public at large, as the oldest federal cultural institution and the largest library in the world ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Library_of_Congress?utm_source=openai)). Its CRS is respected for its impartial research. It has been instrumental in national programs like American Memory, National Film and Recording Registers, and digitization efforts—underscoring its public service mission ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Library_of_Congress?utm_source=openai)). While criticisms arise sometimes in specialized contexts—such as cataloging policy changes noted by librarians—their public discussion demonstrates transparency and institutional responsiveness, not systemic credibility challenges ([reddit.com](https://www.reddit.com/r/Libraries/comments/1q5xs4q/response_to_the_library_of_congress_genreform/?utm_source=openai)).
No major controversies undermine its standing; rather, the LoC is consistently regarded as highly authoritative and trustworthy.
publisher_authority.summary:
The Library of Congress exemplifies high institutional credibility. Owned and funded by the U.S. federal government, it operates with notable financial and organizational transparency, bolstered by independent audits and an Inspector General’s oversight. Its editorial standards—while centered on archival, cataloging, and research functions rather than journalism—reflect accuracy, procedural rigor, and public accountability. As a nonpartisan entity serving Congress and the public, it exhibits minimal to no ideological bias. Its factual output, particularly via the Congressional Research Service and archival holdings, is recognized as authoritative and accurate. Among professionals, scholars, and the general public, the Library of Congress holds an eminent reputation as a cornerstone of American intellectual infrastructure.
Credibility Rating: High. Readers can trust its outputs and collections. Key considerations: while not a news outlet, its practices in cataloging and research stand up to rigorous standards, and occasional policy changes (e.g., in cataloging) may prompt community dialogue but do not detract from its overall reliability.

raw_metadata.title: Unconventional Theories about the Earth’s Shape: Both Ancient and Modern | Worlds Revealed
raw_metadata.description: For centuries, there have been unconventional beliefs about the shape of the Earth. This post provides a historical overview of alternative theories about the Earth's form.
